summary:
Altamira is a cave in the Cantabria region of northern Spain containing Palaeolithic paintings primarily dated to approximately 18,500–14,000 BCE, and widely regarded as a masterpiece of prehistoric art. The cave became famous after the young daughter of amateur archaeologist Marcelino Sanz de Sautuola noticed the paintings in 1879 — her discovery was initially dismissed as a fraud but eventually accepted as authentic, fundamentally transforming understanding of the artistic capacity of Ice Age humans.

The ceiling of Altamira's main chamber bears polychrome paintings of bison — depicted using the natural contours of the rock to suggest three-dimensional form — of such sophistication and artistic skill that they have been called "the Sistine Chapel of Palaeolithic art." Altamira, together with [[event:3400|Lascaux]] in France and dozens of other sites in the Franco-Cantabrian region, represents the peak of European Upper Palaeolithic art associated primarily with the Magdalenian culture. By 10,000–8,000 BCE, the period of this era, the Franco-Cantabrian cave painting tradition had ended, succeeded by the more abstract and less elaborately painted art of the [[event:1280|Azilian culture]] that replaced Magdalenian lifeways following the [[event:1262|climatic changes of the early Holocene]].
